title: practical english usage - Part 14
content:
to give an additional or different meaning. Examples: ex-wife, anti-British, prefix  a form like ex-, anti- or un-, which  can be added to the front of a word attributive.

position. Examples: The house is enormous; She looks happy. See also predicative  Adjectives placed after a verb like be, seem, look are in predicative in The people invited all came late. See also premodifier.

postmodifier  a word that comes after the word which  it modifies, e.g. invited used without following nouns.

often called ’ possessive adjectives', but this is not correct.) Mine, yours, etc are before nouns, so they are not only pronouns, but also determiners. (They are are also possessive pronouns, for the same reason. My, your, etc are used for 'the speaker’ s', 'the hearer’ s', 'that person’ s’ , etc). Mine, yours, his, hers, etc possessive pronoun  My, your, his, her, etc are possessive pronouns (they stand John's; our; mine.

possessive  a form used to show possession and similar ideas. Examples: Examples: we; buses; children; are; many; these. See also singular.

plural  a grammatical form used to refer to more than one person or thing.

Examples: fill up; run over, take in.

phrasal verb  a verb form that is made up of two parts: verb + adverb particle.

tired; the silly old woman; would have been repaired; in the country.

phrase  two or more words that function together as a group. Examples: dead Language terminology •  xxvii thick (= stupid); lose one's cool (= get upset); sparks (= electrician).

informal speech, often in the usage of particular groups of people. Examples: slang  a word, expression or special use of language found mainly in very plural.

uncountable quantity or mass. Examples: me; bus; water; is; much;  this. See also singular  a grammatical form used to talk about one person, thing, etc, or about an simple  a verb form that is not progressive.

verb in the affirmative. Examples: He goes there often; I know; I like chocolate.

simple present (or present simple)  a present verb form that has no auxiliary affirmative. Examples: I stopped; You heard; We knew.

simple past (or past simple)  a past verb tense that has no auxiliary verb in the ' Who's ready for more?' I am '.

auxiliary be or have). Examples: 'Has anybody phoned the police?' 'Jack has.'; short answer  an answer consisting of a subject and an auxiliary verb (or non­ ends with a full stop, question mark or exclamation mark.

has at least one subject and verb. In writing, it begins with a capital letter and question or exclamation. A  sentence consists of one or more clauses, and usually sentence  a group of words that typically expresses a statement, command, second person  see person.

spend the weekend in London.' 'Have you, dear?' to a statement, for instance, to express interest. Example: ‘ I've been invited to reply question  a question (similar in structure to a question tag) used to reply day when we met?

to buy my car, This is the room which needs painting; Do you remember the sometimes also when, where and why. Examples: There's the man who wants Who, whom, whose, which and that can be used as relative pronouns, and relative pronoun  a pronoun used to connect a relative clause to its noun.

See also identifying relative clause, non-identifying relative clause.

relative pronoun like who or which. Example: Hike people who like me.

relative clause  a clause which  modifies a noun, usually introduced by a regular past tense; cats is a regular plural. See also irregular.

regular  following the normal rules or having the usual form. Hoped is a this morning. See also emphatic pronoun.

reflexive pronoun  myself, yourself, himself, etc. Example: I cut myself shaving sentence. Examples: You don't eat meat, do you?; It's a nice day, isn't it?

verb (or non-auxiliary be or have) + pronoun subject, put on to the end of a question tag  an expression like do you? or isn't it?, consisting of an auxiliary phrase to show how  much or how  many we  are talking about.

quantifier  a determiner like many, few, little, several, which  is used in a noun the European Union.
